questions.question: Explain the difference between dense index and sparse index with an example.
keyPoints: Dense index has index entry for every record in data file
Sparse index has index entry for only some records (e.g., first record of each block)
Dense index requires more space but faster lookup
Sparse index requires less space but needs additional search within block
Example: In a student database, dense index on roll number stores entry for each student; sparse index stores entry for first student of each page

questions.question: Why is B+ tree preferred over B-tree for database indexing?
keyPoints: B+ tree stores all data only at leaf nodes
Internal nodes only contain keys (more can fit in memory)
Leaf nodes are linked for efficient range queries
Higher fanout means shallower tree depth
All leaf nodes at same level ensure balanced performance
More efficient for disk-based access patterns

questions.question: Describe the operations performed when inserting a record into a B+ tree.
keyPoints: Find appropriate leaf node for the key value
Insert record in sorted order within leaf
If leaf overflows (more than m entries), split into two nodes
Copy middle key to parent node
If internal node overflows, recursively split and adjust pointers
Update all relevant parent pointers

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of using indexes in databases?
keyPoints: Advantages: Faster data retrieval, efficient sorting, supports query optimization, enables primary key enforcement
Disadvantages: Additional disk space, slower INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE (index maintenance), increased complexity
Trade-off: Use indexes on frequently queried columns with high selectivity

questions.question: Explain the concept of multi-level indexing with a diagram description.
keyPoints: First level is a sparse index on the data file
Second level is an index on the first level index
Process reduces disk I/O by keeping higher levels in memory
Similar to directory system: main index → sub-index → actual data
First level index is small enough to fit in memory
Example: ISAM uses this approach

questions.question: Differentiate between primary index, secondary index, and clustered index.
keyPoints: Primary index: on primary key, unique, sorted, sparse or dense
Secondary index: on non-key fields, can have duplicates, always dense
Clustered index: determines physical order of data, only one per table
Primary and clustered often coincide on primary key
Secondary indexes are always non-clustered
Clustered index changes actual data arrangement

questions.question: Construct a B+ tree of order 3 by inserting the following keys in sequence: 10, 20, 5, 6, 12, 30, 7, 17. Show the tree after each split.
keyPoints: Order 3 means max 3 pointers, min 2 pointers (ceil(3/2)=2)
Insert 10: root with one key [10]
Insert 20: root [10, 20]
Insert 5: root [5, 10, 20] (three keys, overflow)
Split: left [5,10], middle 10, right [20]
Continue inserting remaining keys showing splits
Final tree should be balanced with all leaves at same level
Show how middle keys propagate to parent

questions.question: Compare sequential file organization, direct file organization, and indexed file organization with respect to insertion, deletion, search, and range query operations.
keyPoints: Sequential: Good for full scan and range queries, poor for insertion/deletion (requires rewriting)
Direct/Hash: Excellent for point search (O(1)), poor for range queries, collision issues
Indexed: Good for all operations, range queries especially with B+ tree, overhead of index maintenance
Time complexities: Sequential search O(n), Binary search O(log n), Hash O(1), Index O(log n)
Trade-offs: Space, speed, maintenance cost
Recommend based on query patterns

questions.question: Explain the hash indexing technique. Discuss collision handling methods and the advantages and limitations of hash indexes.
keyPoints: Hash function maps key values to bucket addresses
Static hashing: fixed number of buckets, may overflow
Dynamic hashing: grows/shrinks with data (extendible, linear)
Collision handling: Chaining (linked list per bucket), Open addressing (linear/quadratic probing, rehashing)
Advantages: O(1) average search time, good for equality queries
Limitations: No range query support, performance degrades with collisions, extra space for buckets

questions.question: What is a composite index? How does column order affect composite index usage? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Composite index is an index on two or more columns
Columns stored in order: first column primary sort, second within equal first values
Leftmost prefix rule: index helps queries using first column or first+second
Example: Index on (city, age)
Query WHERE city='Delhi' uses index (leading column)
Query WHERE age=25 does NOT use index (no leading column)
Query WHERE city='Delhi' AND age=30 uses full index
Order matters: put high-cardinality column first for better selectivity
